diagrams.title: Biological Neuron to Artificial Neuron Mapping
diagrams.description: A side-by-side comparison showing how the biological neuron inspired the artificial neuron. The left side shows a biological neuron with dendrites (receiving signals), cell body/soma (processing), and axon (sending output). The right side shows the artificial neuron model with inputs corresponding to dendrites, weighted sum and activation function corresponding to the soma, and output corresponding to the axon signal.
diagrams.purpose: Helps learners understand the inspiration behind artificial neurons by mapping biological components to their computational equivalents, making the abstract concept more intuitive and grounded in neuroscience.

diagrams.title: Perceptron Model Architecture
diagrams.description: A detailed diagram of a single perceptron showing the complete forward pass computation. Multiple input features (x1, x2, x3, ..., xn) are shown flowing into the perceptron, each multiplied by its corresponding weight (w1, w2, w3, ..., wn). A bias term (b) is added to the weighted sum. The result passes through an activation function (commonly step, sigmoid, or ReLU), producing the final output. The diagram includes mathematical notation: z = Σ(wi * xi) + b, and y = f(z).
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental computation performed by a perceptron - the basic building block of neural networks. This visualizes how inputs are transformed into outputs through weighted connections and non-linear activation, forming the foundation for understanding more complex deep learning architectures.

diagrams.title: Perceptron Decision Boundary Example
diagrams.description: A 2D visualization showing how a perceptron learns to classify data. The diagram displays a scatter plot with two classes of points (e.g., red and blue circles) and the decision boundary (a straight line) that separates them. Arrows indicate the misclassified points and the direction in which the weights would be adjusted. The perceptron learning rule is visualized: w_new = w_old + learning_rate * error * input.
elements: class A data points (e.g., red circles)
class B data points (e.g., blue circles)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates how a perceptron performs binary classification by finding a linear decision boundary. This helps learners visualize the learning process - how the perceptron iteratively adjusts weights to correctly separate different classes of data, which is fundamental to understanding supervised learning in neural networks.
